narrative: As a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several positive ways, including a solid LSAT score of 37—right above the typical applicant's 36.7—and a Year 3 school decile ranking of 7, which is stronger than the average of 5.53. However, the university's admission model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 59.2%, tipping the scales toward rejection to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. This prediction hinged most heavily on your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, which falls slightly below the average of 3.21 and was the biggest factor pushing the model toward forecasting a bar exam struggle. Next in influence was your Year 1 school decile of 5, a bit under the average of 5.71, which further nudged the prediction in the direction of potential failure by signaling early academic challenges. On a brighter note, your stronger Year 3 decile ranking worked against that trend, actively pulling the model's assessment toward a higher chance of passing and showing your improvement over time. Compared to typical students in the dataset, your full-time enrollment aligns with the vast majority at 92.7%, so it didn't sway things much either way. Overall, these academic markers from your undergrad and early schooling years outweighed your strengths, as the model weighs them as the strongest signals for bar success. While this decision feels disappointing, especially given your LSAT performance and family stability, it reflects a data-driven focus on patterns from past students. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing a post-baccalaureate program to boost your GPA, as even modest gains there could shift future predictions dramatically. You might also gather letters highlighting your Year 3 turnaround to humanize your application elsewhere. Many applicants in similar positions reapply successfully after targeted improvements—your upward trajectory suggests you have that potential. Keep pushing; law school doors aren't closed forever.
